questions.prompt: You're scoping v1 of an autonomous assistant. The team wants to support 'knowledge workers' broadly with 12 tool integrations. Right move?

choices.text: Build all 12 integrations and ship to everyone.

Scope hard: one specific persona, one job-to-be-done, 2–5 tools, level 3 autonomy. 'Knowledge workers' is too broad to design for; specific personas drive specific decisions. Better to ship a great narrow assistant than a mediocre wide one. Expand after v1 wins.
Use Opus to make up for breadth.
Add voice + mobile + multi-user to compete.

questions.explanation: Generalist v1s nearly always fail. Pick one persona (executive coaches, engineering managers, solo founders), one JTBD (pre-meeting prep, daily briefing, inbox triage), 2–5 connectors, level 3 autonomy. Earn expansion through wins.

questions.prompt: What's the typical right autonomy level for an assistant's v1?

choices.text: Level 3 — propose then approve. Lower trust requirement, easier debugging, smaller eval surface, faster iteration with real users. Level 4 (scheduled) is the next step once level 3 is loved; level 5 is a later phase requiring strong trust and evals.

questions.explanation: Level 3 is the canonical v1 autonomy: useful (proactive proposals) without dangerous (no unilateral irreversible actions). The path to higher autonomy goes through proving level 3 works for your users in your scope.
